```{r}
    .groups       = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Total))

knitr::kable(
  partner_revenue %>%
    mutate(
      Rev_Current   = paste0("$", formatC(Rev_Current,   format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")),
      Rev_At_Target = paste0("$", formatC(Rev_At_Target, format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")),
      Rev_Uplift    = paste0("$", formatC(Rev_Uplift,    format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ","))
    ),
  caption = "Revenue by Partner: Current vs Target CPL"
)

ggplot(partner_revenue, aes(x = reorder(Partner, Total))) +
  geom_col(aes(y = Rev_At_Target / 1000), fill = "#1a9850", alpha = 0.5, width = 0.6) +
  geom_col(aes(y = Rev_Current   / 1000), fill = "#2C7BB6", width = 0.4) +
  geom_text(aes(y = Rev_Current / 1000,
                label = paste0(Closed_Rate, "% closed")),
            hjust = -0.1, size = 3.2) +
  coord_flip() +
  labs(
    title    = "Revenue by Partner: Current CPL (blue) vs Target CPL (green)",
    subtitle = "Green = potential revenue at $33 CPL if 9.6% closed rate achieved",
    x        = NULL,
    y        = "Revenue ($000s)"
  ) +
  theme_analyst

ggsave(
  "C:/Users/sgpat/Documents/LeadQuality_CaseStudy/output/charts/chart_revenue_impact.png",
  width = 8, height = 5, dpi = 150
)

```
Google generates $48,000 of the total $85,710 in current revenue (56.0%), making it the largest revenue contributor despite its below-target quality rate of 7.6%. If Google reaches the 9.6% target, its revenue at the $33 CPL would be $52,800, an uplift of $4,800. Yahoo adds another $2,847 in potential uplift. AdKnowledge, despite its superior quality at 12.3%, contributes only $513 in potential uplift due to its low volume of 171 leads. This table makes the strategic tension explicit: Google is both the biggest problem and the biggest revenue driver. Any strategy that aggressively cuts Google traffic without replacing it with quality volume from AdKnowledge would reduce total revenue even as it raises quality rate.


## Business Impact Summary
```{r}
biz_summary <- tibble(
  Item  = c(
    "6-Month Lead Volume (non-duplicate)",
    "Current Closed Rate",
    "Assignment Target Closed Rate",
    "Closed Leads Gap to Target",
    "Current 6-Month Revenue @ $30 CPL",
    "Target 6-Month Revenue @ $33 CPL",
    "6-Month Revenue Uplift",
    "Annualised Revenue Uplift (x2)",
    "Largest Underperforming Segment",
    "Highest Performing Segment"
  ),
  Value = c(
    formatC(total_base,    format = "d", big.mark = ","),
    paste0(closed_rate,    "%"),
    paste0(q_target,       "%"),
    formatC(closed_gap,    format = "d", big.mark = ","),
    paste0("$", formatC(revenue_current,    format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")),
    paste0("$", formatC(revenue_target,     format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")),
    paste0("$", formatC(revenue_uplift,     format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")),
    paste0("$", formatC(revenue_uplift * 2, format = "f", digits = 0, big.mark = ",")),
    "Google + Over 100K Debt",
    "AdKnowledge + 70K-90K Debt"
  )
)

knitr::kable(biz_summary,
             caption = "Phase 8: Business Impact Summary")

```
The complete business case is: 2,857 leads, 231 Closed, 8.09% closed rate, 44 additional Closed leads needed, $8,571 in 6-month revenue uplift, $17,142 annualised. The largest underperforming segment is Google + Over 100K. The highest performing segment is AdKnowledge + 70K-90K. This summary table is the single table a stakeholder needs to understand the financial stakes of the recommendations.


# Phase 9: Recommendations


## Recommendation Framework
```{r}
rec_framework <- tibble(
  Recommendation = c(
    "R1: Reduce Google Over 100K traffic",
    "R2: Reallocate budget from Head3 widget",
    "R3: Scale AdKnowledge spend",
    "R4: Implement Verify Score pre-filter",
    "R5: Prioritise 70K-90K debt band targeting"
  ),
  Evidence = c(
    "Google + Over 100K = 4.7% quality, lowest cell at volume",
    "Head3 = 6.9% quality, only widget below target at volume",
    "AdKnowledge = 18.7% quality vs 11.2% Google, 7.5pp gap",
    "Mann-Whitney p=0.0003: higher verify score = better quality",
    "70K-90K = 20.2% quality, highest debt band, statistically confirmed"
  ),
  Impact = c("High", "Medium", "High", "Medium", "Medium"),
  Effort = c("Low", "Low", "Medium", "High", "Medium"),
  Priority = c(1L, 2L, 3L, 4L, 5L)
)

knitr::kable(rec_framework,
             caption = "Phase 9: Recommendation Priority Framework")

ggplot(rec_framework,
       aes(x = Effort, y = Impact,
           label = paste0("R", Priority))) +
  geom_point(aes(color = factor(Priority)), size = 12, alpha = 0.8) +
  geom_text(color = "white", fontface = "bold", size = 4) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("1" = "#d73027", "2" = "#fc8d59",
               "3" = "#1a9850", "4" = "#2C7BB6",
               "5" = "#756bb1"),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  scale_x_discrete(limits = c("Low", "Medium", "High")) +
  scale_y_discrete(limits = c("Low", "Medium", "High")) +
  labs(
    title    = "Recommendation Priority Matrix: Impact vs Effort",
    subtitle = "R1-R5 plotted by implementation effort and expected quality impact",
    x = "Implementation Effort", y = "Business Impact"
  ) +
  theme_analyst

ggsave(
  "C:/Users/sgpat/Documents/LeadQuality_CaseStudy/output/charts/chart_priority_matrix.png",
  width = 8, height = 6, dpi = 150
)

```
Five recommendations are structured by priority, with R1 and R3 rated High Impact and R1 and R2 rated Low Effort, placing them in the top-right quadrant of the impact-effort matrix. The framework is evidence-anchored: every recommendation cites a specific phase, metric, and p-value or percentage point gap. R4 (verify score filter) is the only High Effort item, which reflects the reality that deploying a real-time API-based lead scoring filter requires system integration work that the other four recommendations do not.
​

## Recommendation 1: Scale AdKnowledge, Reduce Google Over 100K
```{r}
r1_data <- base_data %>%
  filter(Partner %in% c("Google", "AdKnowledge")) %>%
  group_by(Partner, DebtBand) %>%
  summarise(
    Total       = n(),
    Good        = sum(QualityGroup == "Good"),
    Closed      = sum(CallStatus == "Closed"),
    Quality_Rate = round(Good   / Total * 100, 1),
    Closed_Rate  = round(Closed / Total * 100, 1),
    .groups     = "drop"
  ) %>%
  filter(Total >= 10)

knitr::kable(r1_data,
             caption = "R1 Evidence: Google vs AdKnowledge Quality by Debt Band")

# Quantify the Over 100K Google drag
google_100k <- base_data %>%
  filter(Partner == "Google", DebtBand == "Over 100K") %>%
  summarise(
    Total       = n(),
    Good        = sum(QualityGroup == "Good"),
    Closed      = sum(CallStatus == "Closed"),
    Quality_Rate = round(Good   / Total * 100, 1),
    Closed_Rate  = round(Closed / Total * 100, 1)
  )

cat("Google + Over 100K segment:\n")
cat("  Leads:        ", google_100k$Total, "\n")
cat("  Quality Rate: ", google_100k$Quality_Rate, "%\n")
cat("  Closed Rate:  ", google_100k$Closed_Rate, "%\n")
cat("  vs target:    ", google_100k$Closed_Rate - q_target, "pp below target\n")

```
The R1 evidence table confirms the quality rate asymmetry at every debt band where both Google and AdKnowledge have sufficient volume. Google's Over 100K segment delivers 4.7% quality and 2.8% closed rate from 107 leads, the worst meaningful cell in the dataset at any combination. AdKnowledge, by contrast, achieves 12.3% closed rate overall versus Google's 7.6%, a gap of 4.7 percentage points at partner level. The specific operational recommendation is a budget reallocation: reduce Google's bid multipliers or keyword targeting for debt-related terms that over-index to the Over 100K segment, and redirect that spend to AdKnowledge campaigns targeting the 50K-90K debt range where AdKnowledge's performance advantage is greatest.

## Recommendation 2: Reallocate Widget Budget from Head3
```{r}
r2_data <- base_data %>%
  filter(widget_design %in% c("Head3", "BlueMeter", "Head2", "Standard")) %>%
  group_by(widget_design) %>%
  summarise(
    Total        = n(),
    Good         = sum(QualityGroup == "Good"),
    Closed       = sum(CallStatus == "Closed"),
    Quality_Rate = round(Good   / Total * 100, 1),
    Closed_Rate  = round(Closed / Total * 100, 1),
    .groups      = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(desc(Quality_Rate))

knitr::kable(r2_data,
             caption = "R2 Evidence: Head3 vs Recommended Replacement Widgets")

# If Head3 traffic was redirected to BlueMeter
head3_leads     <- base_data %>% filter(widget_design == "Head3") %>% nrow()
bluemeter_rate  <- base_data %>%
  filter(widget_design == "BlueMeter") %>%
  summarise(r = mean(QualityGroup == "Good")) %>%
  pull(r)

head3_rate <- mean(base_data$QualityGroup[base_data$widget_design == "Head3"] == "Good",
                   na.rm = TRUE)
extra_good <- round(head3_leads * (bluemeter_rate - head3_rate))

cat("Head3 leads that could be redirected:", head3_leads, "\n")
cat("BlueMeter quality rate:", round(bluemeter_rate * 100, 1), "%\n")
cat("Head3 quality rate:", round(head3_rate * 100, 1), "%\n")
cat("Estimated additional Good leads if redirected to BlueMeter:", extra_good, "\n")

cat("Head3 leads that could be redirected:", head3_leads, "\n")
cat("BlueMeter quality rate:", round(bluemeter_rate * 100, 1), "%\n")
cat("Head3 quality rate:",
    round(mean(base_data$QualityGroup[base_data$widget_design == "Head3"] == "Good") * 100, 1),
    "%\n")

```
Head3 delivers a 5.6% closed rate and 6.9% quality rate from 72 leads, the only widget below the 9.6% target with meaningful volume. Head2 at 12.8% closed rate and BlueMeter at 10.3% are the two direct replacements. The action is a creative swap, not a volume change: impressions currently served by the Head3 widget are redirected to Head2 or BlueMeter without changing total lead volume. This is classified as Low Effort because it is a campaign configuration change, not a structural change to bidding strategy or data systems.
​

## Recommendation 3: Implement Verify Score Pre-Filter
```{r}
r3_data <- base_data %>%
  filter(!is.na(verify_score)) %>%
  mutate(
    score_band = case_when(
      verify_score >= 4.5 ~ "High (4.5-5.0)",
      verify_score >= 3.0 ~ "Mid (3.0-4.4)",
      TRUE                ~ "Low (1.5-2.9)"
    ),
    score_band = factor(score_band,
                        levels = c("Low (1.5-2.9)",
                                   "Mid (3.0-4.4)",
                                   "High (4.5-5.0)"))
  ) %>%
  group_by(score_band) %>%
  summarise(
    Total        = n(),
    Good         = sum(QualityGroup == "Good"),
    Closed       = sum(CallStatus == "Closed"),
    Quality_Rate = round(Good   / Total * 100, 1),
    Closed_Rate  = round(Closed / Total * 100, 1),
    .groups      = "drop"
  )

knitr::kable(r3_data,
             caption = "R3 Evidence: Quality Rate by Verify Score Band")

ggplot(r3_data, aes(x = score_band, y = Quality_Rate, fill = score_band)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.6) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = q_target, linetype = "dashed",
             color = "red", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = paste0(Quality_Rate, "%")),
            vjust = -0.5, size = 4, fontface = "bold") +
  scale_fill_manual(
    values = c("Low (1.5-2.9)"  = "#d73027",
               "Mid (3.0-4.4)"  = "#fee08b",
               "High (4.5-5.0)" = "#1a9850"),
    guide = "none"
  ) +
  labs(
    title    = "Quality Rate by Verify Score Band",
    subtitle = "Dashed line = 9.6% target | Evidence for R3 pre-filter recommendation",
    x = NULL, y = "Quality Rate (%)"
  ) +
  theme_analyst

```
The verify score analysis confirms a statistically significant quality gradient across score bands. The Mann-Whitney result (p = 0.0003) is the strongest statistical signal in the entire Phase 7 analysis, stronger than even the Debt Band chi-square result. Good leads have a median verify_score of 5.0 versus 4.0 for Bad leads.The operational recommendation is to set a minimum verify_score threshold of 4.5 as a real-time lead acceptance gate. A threshold of 3.0 is not defensible because Bad leads already have a median of 4.0, meaning most Bad leads would pass through unchecked. A threshold of 4.5 sits between the Good median (5.0) and Bad median (4.0), maximising separation between quality groups while preserving the majority of Good leads. The constraint is that 1,628 leads (53.9%) currently lack a verify_score because PhoneScore was introduced partway through the data collection period. Full deployment requires the verify score to be available at submission time for all new leads, which requires a data pipeline confirmation that both AddressScore and PhoneScore are being populated in real time.


## Recommendation 4: Prioritise 70K–90K Debt Band
```{r}
r4_data <- base_data %>%
  group_by(DebtBand) %>%
  summarise(
    Total        = n(),
    Good         = sum(QualityGroup == "Good"),
    Closed       = sum(CallStatus == "Closed"),
    Quality_Rate = round(Good   / Total * 100, 1),
    Closed_Rate  = round(Closed / Total * 100, 1),
    Pct_of_Total = round(Total / nrow(base_data) * 100, 1),
    .groups      = "drop"
  ) %>%
  arrange(DebtBand) %>%
  mutate(Status = if_else(Quality_Rate >= q_target, "Above Target", "Below Target"))

knitr::kable(r4_data,
             caption = "R4 Evidence: Quality Rate and Volume by Debt Band")

cat("70K-90K debt band share of total leads:",
    r4_data$Pct_of_Total[r4_data$DebtBand == "70K-90K"], "%\n")
cat("Quality rate uplift vs baseline:",
    r4_data$Quality_Rate[r4_data$DebtBand == "70K-90K"] - 13.0,
    "pp above overall baseline\n")

```
The 70K-90K debt band achieves 14.5% closed rate and 20.2% quality rate from 124 leads, the highest closed rate of any debt band by a margin of 4.2 percentage points over the next best (90K-100K at 10.3%). It is 4.9 percentage points above the 9.6% target. The Debt Band chi-square result (p = 0.001) provides the statistical confirmation that this difference is not random. The recommendation is to adjust keyword bidding and ad copy language to attract users in the $70,000 to $90,000 debt range, which in practice means targeting terms like "debt consolidation over 70000" or "settling large debt" rather than generic "debt relief" terms that disproportionately attract lower-debt, lower-quality leads.



## Recommendation 5: Replicate Call Center Pre-Screening Online
```{r}
r5_data <- base_data %>%
  group_by(lead_source) %>%
  summarise(
    Total         = n(),
    Good          = sum(QualityGroup == "Good"),
    Closed        = sum(CallStatus == "Closed"),
    Bad           = sum(QualityGroup == "Bad"),
    No_Outcome    = sum(QualityGroup == "No Outcome"),
    Quality_Rate  = round(Good   / Total * 100, 1),
    Closed_Rate   = round(Closed / Total * 100, 1),
    No_Outcome_Rt = round(No_Outcome / Total * 100, 1),
    .groups       = "drop"
  )

knitr::kable(r5_data,
             caption = "R5 Evidence: Call Center vs Online Full Quality Profile")

cat("Call Center No Outcome rate:",
    r5_data$No_Outcome_Rt[r5_data$lead_source == "Call Center"], "%\n")
cat("Online No Outcome rate:",
    r5_data$No_Outcome_Rt[r5_data$lead_source == "Online"], "%\n")
cat("No Outcome gap (pre-screening effect):",
    r5_data$No_Outcome_Rt[r5_data$lead_source == "Online"] -
      r5_data$No_Outcome_Rt[r5_data$lead_source == "Call Center"],
    "pp\n")

```

The R5 evidence table shows Call Center's No Outcome rate is materially lower than Online's, which is the true mechanism behind Call Center's higher headline quality rate. When No Outcome leads are excluded and only contacted leads are compared (Phase 7, Section 8.6), Call Center and Online have virtually identical conversion rates (47.1% vs 45.5%). The Call Center advantage is entirely about pre-screening before entry, not about better conversion once contacted. The operational recommendation is to introduce a pre-submission qualification question online, such as a debt amount confirmation slider or a minimum income question, that mimics the verbal pre-screening a call center agent performs. This is the only recommendation that requires a product/UX change to the lead form itself.


## Priority Action Table
```{r}
action_table <- tibble(
  Priority    = 1:5,
  Action      = c(
    "Stop/reduce Google ads targeting Over 100K debt segment",
    "Shift Head3 widget impressions to BlueMeter or Head2",
    "Increase AdKnowledge budget, focus on 50K-90K debt bands",
    "Deploy verifyscore 4.5 as real-time lead acceptance filter",
    "Adjust keyword/ad copy to attract 70K-90K debt profile"
  ),
  Impact_Type = c(
    "Remove drag: lifts overall closed rate immediately",
    "Remove drag: replaces 6.9% widget with 14.4%+ widget",
    "Add quality volume: 18.7% vs 11.2% for Google",
    "Reduce bad leads at submission: improves pipeline quality",
    "Shift lead mix toward highest-quality debt segment"
  ),
  Stat_Support = c(
    "Phase 6: 4.7% quality at 107 leads, lowest cell at volume",
    "Phase 5: Head3 only widget below target with n>50",
    "Phase 7: Partner borderline sig (p=0.079), 7.5pp practical gap",
    "Phase 7: Mann-Whitney p=0.0003, strongest stat result",
    "Phase 7: DebtBand chi-sq p=0.001, only sig variable"
  ),
  Timeline    = c(
    "Immediate (budget reallocation)",
    "Immediate (creative swap)",
    "30 days (campaign expansion)",
    "60 days (API/system integration)",
    "30 days (keyword bid adjustments)"
  )
)

knitr::kable(action_table,
             caption = "Phase 9: Prioritised Action Plan with Statistical Evidence")

```
The five actions are sequenced correctly for implementation: R1 and R2 are immediate campaign configuration changes executable within days, R3 and R5 require 30-day campaign and keyword adjustments, and R4 requires 60-day system integration for the real-time verify score filter. Together, if R1 (remove worst segment), R2 (remove worst widget), and R3 (scale AdKnowledge) are implemented simultaneously, the combined effect is estimated to close the majority of the 44-lead gap to the 9.6% target, with R4 and R5 providing the remaining push and long-term quality floor.
